skill.name: web-artifacts-builder
skill.description: Build complex Claude HTML artifacts as React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui projects, then bundle them into one shareable HTML file.
skill.summary: Create and bundle multi-component React artifacts for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity weak-cryptography hits were false positives caused by Tailwind color tokens and descriptive text, not cryptographic operations. The skill has no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection, but it carries medium operational risk because its scripts install packages, create and overwrite project files, extract a local archive, and delete build outputs.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation and Build Commands
medium_findings.description: The setup and bundling workflow runs pnpm, npm, Parcel, and tar commands. This is expected for a frontend artifact builder, but it executes third-party package manager behavior and should run only in a trusted workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and are core behavior of the skill. I found no evidence that they exfiltrate data, but package installation and build scripts are real execution and supply-chain risk.

medium_findings.title: Generated Project Files Are Created and Overwritten
medium_findings.description: The initialization script writes configuration and source files, edits Vite defaults, updates TypeScript configuration, and extracts UI components. This is normal for a scaffold tool but can overwrite files in the generated project directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes and archive extraction are explicit and intentional. The risk is bounded to the generated project path when used as documented, so this is medium rather than high.

low_findings.title: Shell Invocation Findings Are Mostly Expected Workflow Commands
low_findings.description: The detected shell invocations and command substitutions support version detection, script path discovery, artifact setup, and bundle size reporting. Project name variables are quoted, reducing command-injection risk, although unusual project names may still break generated configuration text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plain setup instructions and shell metadata operations, not hidden execution paths. Confidence is below very high because generated text still depends on a user-supplied project name.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain Tailwind color keys, HSL design tokens, or prose. I found no hashing, encryption, signing, password storage, or cryptographic primitive in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected context shows CSS color configuration and skill description text only. There is no semantic evidence of cryptographic behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Documentation Metadata
low_findings.description: The hardcoded shadcn/ui URL appears in a generated components configuration schema reference and in the skill reference section. It is not used for credential transfer or runtime data upload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is visible as documentation or schema metadata. I found no network request code using this URL.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alert Is a Configuration Comment
low_findings.description: The dynamic import pattern is part of a Tailwind configuration type comment inside a generated file template. It does not execute dynamic JavaScript imports.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is embedded in a heredoc that writes a Tailwind config comment. It is not an import expression evaluated by the shell script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill executes npm and pnpm commands that can download packages and run package lifecycle behavior. This is legitimate for scaffolding, but users should run it in a disposable or trusted workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package manager commands are explicit and central to the script. The risk comes from normal package execution, not from hidden malicious code in the reviewed text files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Build Output Deletion
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundling script removes dist and bundle.html from the current directory before rebuilding. This is expected cleanup, but it can delete user files with those names if the script is run in the wrong directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The rm command and target paths are explicit. The script checks for package.json and index.html first, which lowers but does not remove misuse risk.

content.value_statement: Complex Claude artifacts often need reusable components, state, routing, and polished UI structure. This skill creates a React and shadcn/ui project, then bundles it into one HTML artifact for sharing.
actual_capabilities: Initializes a React 18, TypeScript, and Vite project for artifact development.
Configures Tailwind CSS 3.4.1 with shadcn/ui design tokens and aliases.
Installs common Radix UI and shadcn/ui dependencies for component-rich interfaces.
Extracts a bundled set of shadcn/ui component files into the generated project.
Bundles a completed React app into one self-contained HTML file with inlined assets.
Provides guidance for optional local testing and artifact sharing.
limitations: Requires Node.js 18 or later and access to npm or pnpm package registries.
Best suited to complex artifacts, not simple single-file HTML or JSX snippets.
Runs setup and build scripts that modify files in the project workspace.
Does not guarantee that the final artifact meets Claude artifact size limits.

use_cases.title: Prototype Interactive Product Demos
use_cases.description: Create a component-based React demo with forms, dialogs, tabs, and responsive layouts, then package it as one artifact.

use_cases.title: Build Internal Data Tools
use_cases.description: Scaffold a frontend with tables, filters, charts, and controlled state for quick review in an AI-assisted workflow.

use_cases.title: Prepare Shareable UI Experiments
use_cases.description: Develop a polished React interface locally and bundle it into a portable HTML artifact for collaborators.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Artifact Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new artifact workspace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-artifacts-builder to create a React artifact project named dashboard-demo. Keep the default stack and explain the generated files.

prompt_templates.title: Add shadcn/ui Components
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a component-based interface after initialization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React artifact with tabs, cards, a table, and a dialog using the installed shadcn/ui components. Keep the UI responsive.

prompt_templates.title: Bundle a Finished Artifact
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert a completed project into one shareable HTML file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the artifact bundling workflow for the current project and confirm where the single HTML output is created.

prompt_templates.title: Refine a Complex App Artifact
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for a polished multi-view artifact.
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this React artifact with better state organization, accessible controls, and a production-ready bundle. Note any files changed.

output_examples.input: Create a project for a customer analytics artifact.
output_examples.output: A new React and TypeScript workspace is created with Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui components, path aliases, and development dependencies installed.

output_examples.input: Bundle my completed artifact for sharing.
output_examples.output: The project is built with Parcel, assets are inlined, and a single bundle.html file is produced in the project root.

output_examples.input: Add a polished interface with filters and dialogs.
output_examples.output: The artifact uses reusable React components, shadcn/ui controls, responsive layout structure, and stateful interactions.

best_practices: Run the scripts inside a dedicated project directory or disposable workspace.
Use this skill when the artifact needs multiple components, state, or bundling.
Review the generated bundle size before sharing large or media-heavy artifacts.
anti_patterns: Do not use this workflow for a tiny static HTML snippet.
Do not run the bundling script from an unrelated project root.
Do not skip reviewing package installation output in restricted environments.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui project for complex HTML artifacts.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is useful for Claude artifacts and for coding agents that can edit and run project scripts.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. The setup installs packages from npm or pnpm registries unless dependencies are already cached.

faq.question: What file does the bundle command produce?
faq.answer: It produces bundle.html in the project root after building and inlining the app assets.

faq.question: Is this safe to run in any folder?
faq.answer: No. Run it in a dedicated workspace because it creates, edits, and removes project files.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for simple artifacts that can be written as one HTML or JSX file without a build step.
